questions.to_find: The string printed after modifying a character via pointer arithmetic

understand.plain: A character array s1 is initialized to the string '1234'. A pointer p is set to point two positions ahead (to s1[2], which holds '3'). The character at that position is changed to '0'. When the string is printed, the modified array is output as a C string up to the null terminator.

keywords.explain: Adding an integer n to a pointer moves it forward by n elements of the pointed-to type.
keywords.example: If p = s1 + 2, p points to s1[2].

keywords.explain: A null-terminated sequence of characters; printf with %s prints until '\0'.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='130'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='60' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='46' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>'1'</text><text x='40' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>[0]</text><rect x='80' y='20' width='60' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='110' y='46' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>'2'</text><text x='110' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>[1]</text><rect x='150' y='20' width='60' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='180' y='46' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>'0'</text><text x='180' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>[2]</text><rect x='220' y='20' width='60' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='46' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>'4'</text><text x='250' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>[3]</text><rect x='290' y='20' width='60' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='320' y='46' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>'\0'</text><text x='320' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>[4]</text><text x='180' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>p = s1+2 then *p='0'</text><line x1='180' y1='95' x2='180' y2='63' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='4' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L8,4 L0,8 Z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Memory layout of s1 showing indices 0-4 with values '1','2','0','4','\0' after the pointer write. Index 2 is highlighted as the position modified by p.

given.aim: Determine the output string after modifying one character using a pointer.

terms.connects: Provides the base string that gets partially modified.

terms.meaning: Pointer p is set to address of s1[2]
terms.example: If s1 is at address 1000, p = 1002 (each char is 1 byte).
terms.connects: Identifies which element will be overwritten.

terms.meaning: The character at the location p points to (s1[2]) is set to '0'
terms.connects: This is the modification that changes the printed output.

given.plan: Trace the pointer arithmetic to find which index is modified, update the array, then read off the string up to the null terminator.

steps.title: Map pointer to array index
steps.formula_raw: p = s1 + 2 => p points to s1[2]
steps.apply: s1 is declared as char s1[7] = "1234". Its contents are: s1[0]='1', s1[1]='2', s1[2]='3', s1[3]='4', s1[4]='\0'. p = s1 + 2 makes p point to s1[2].
steps.note: Pointer arithmetic on a char array advances by 1 byte per index.

steps.title: Apply the assignment and read string
steps.note: The null terminator at s1[4] remains unchanged, so the string ends after '4'.

solution.result: The program prints 1204. Correct answer: C. 1204

questions.formula_note: Pointer arithmetic on arrays: (array + n) points to array[n]; dereferencing modifies that element directly.

questions.to_find: The integer value printed by ip[1] given ip = arr + 4

understand.plain: An integer array arr has 13 elements. A pointer ip is set to arr+4, meaning it points to the 5th element (index 4). The expression ip[1] accesses the element one position after ip, which is arr[5].

keywords.explain: ip = arr + 4 makes ip point to arr[4]. Then ip[1] is equivalent to *(ip + 1) = arr[5].
keywords.example: If ip points to arr[4], ip[0]=arr[4], ip[1]=arr[5], ip[2]=arr[6].

keywords.explain: ip[k] is syntactic sugar for *(ip + k); it accesses the element k positions after where ip points.

understand.visual_alt: arr array with 13 elements. ip points to index 4 (value 5). ip[1] points to index 5 (value 6).

given.aim: Find the value of ip[1] when ip is a pointer into the middle of an integer array.

terms.connects: Provides the data from which ip[1] is read.
Sets the base from which ip[1] is computed.

terms.meaning: Element one position after ip, i.e., arr[5]
terms.connects: This is the value that gets printed.

given.plan: Identify where ip points (arr+4 = arr[4]). Then ip[1] = arr[4+1] = arr[5]. Look up arr[5] in the initializer.

steps.title: Identify pointer base
steps.formula_raw: ip = arr + 4 => ip points to arr[4]
steps.apply: arr is {1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9,0,1,2,5}. Index 4 has value 5. So ip points to the element with value 5.
steps.note: arr+4 is not value 4 but the address of the 5th element (0-indexed).

steps.title: Compute ip[1]
steps.apply: arr[5] = 6 (the 6th element in the initializer list: 1,2,3,4,5,6,...). So printf prints 6.
steps.note: Subscript notation on a pointer is identical to pointer arithmetic: ip[k] = *(ip+k).

questions.formula_note: Pointer-array equivalence: ip[k] = *(ip+k); when ip = arr+n, ip[k] = arr[n+k].

questions.to_find: The integer value printed by the nested pointer expression *(*(a + **a + 2) + 3)

understand.plain: A 4x5 integer matrix is declared. The expression uses multi-level pointer arithmetic: **a gives the value at row 0, column 0 (which is 1). This value is used as an offset to select a row, and then another offset selects the column. The final value is printed.

keywords.explain: **a dereferences twice: *a gives a[0] (pointer to first row), then **a gives a[0][0].
a is a pointer to rows; a+n points to row n. *(a+n) is a pointer to the first element of row n.

understand.visual_alt: 4x5 matrix with rows 0-3. The expression evaluates to row 3, column 3 which is 19, highlighted in the table.

given.aim: Evaluate the complex nested pointer expression on a 2D array and determine the printed value.

terms.meaning: a[0][0], the first element of the 2D array
terms.connects: Used as the row offset in the outer pointer expression.

terms.example: *(a+3) is equivalent to a[3], pointing to {16,17,18,19,20}.
terms.connects: Selects which row to index into.

terms.meaning: Pointer to a[3][0] advanced by 3, i.e., pointing to a[3][3]
terms.connects: The final dereference gives the printed value.

given.plan: Evaluate inside-out: compute **a to get the integer 1, substitute into the row expression a+1+2=a+3 to get row 3, then add column offset 3 to get element a[3][3]=19.

steps.title: Evaluate **a
steps.apply: In C, a 2D array name a decays to a pointer to its first row. *a = a[0] is a pointer to the first element of row 0. **a = a[0][0] = 1.
steps.note: **a gives the integer value 1, not a pointer.

steps.title: Compute the row pointer: a + **a + 2
steps.apply: Since **a = 1, the expression a + **a + 2 = a + 3. This is a pointer to row 3 of the matrix.

steps.title: Dereference to get column pointer and final value
steps.apply: *(a+3) = a[3] is a pointer to the first element of row 3. Adding 3 gives a pointer to a[3][3]. The outer * dereferences it: a[3][3] = 19.
steps.note: Row 3 is {16,17,18,19,20}, so index 3 within it is 19.

questions.formula_note: 2D array pointer arithmetic: a[i][j] = *(*(a+i)+j); **a = a[0][0] gives the integer value at the first element.

questions.to_find: The correct code to replace X so that matrix M is transposed in-place

understand.plain: To transpose a matrix in-place, we must swap M[i][j] with M[j][i] for all pairs where i != j. The key insight is that we should only swap upper-triangular elements (j starts from i, not 0) to avoid swapping back elements that were already swapped. Option A starts j from 0 which would swap each pair twice, undoing the transpose. Option C starts j from i, correctly swapping only above the diagonal.

keywords.explain: Swapping element at row i, column j with element at row j, column i for all i,j.
Using a temporary variable t to exchange two values without extra array allocation.
keywords.example: t=M[i][j]; M[i][j]=M[j][i]; M[j][i]=t; correctly swaps the two elements.

keywords.explain: Starting j from i (not 0) ensures each off-diagonal pair is swapped exactly once.

understand.visual_alt: 4x4 grid showing which cells are swapped (upper triangle, purple), which are on the diagonal (no swap needed), and which are skipped (lower triangle, already handled by mirror swap).

given.aim: Find the correct loop body to transpose a 4x4 matrix in-place by swapping M[i][j] with M[j][i] exactly once for each off-diagonal pair.

terms.meaning: Iterates over each row of the matrix
terms.connects: The missing X must be an inner loop over columns j.

terms.meaning: t=M[i][j]; M[i][j]=M[j][i]; M[j][i]=t; is the only correct order
terms.example: Save M[i][j] in t, overwrite M[i][j] with M[j][i], then put t into M[j][i].
terms.connects: Options B and D have incorrect assignment order, overwriting values before saving them.

terms.meaning: Starting j at i prevents double-swapping: each pair (i,j) with i<j is visited exactly once
terms.example: Option A starts j from 0, which would swap (0,2) with (2,0) and then swap (2,0) with (0,2) again, undoing the work.
terms.connects: This is the key difference between option A (wrong) and option C (correct).

given.plan: Check correctness of swap order (eliminates B, D), then check whether j starts from 0 or i to ensure each pair is swapped exactly once (eliminates A, confirms C).

steps.title: Eliminate options with wrong swap order (B and D)
steps.apply: Options B and D have: M[i][j]=t (t is uninitialized here), t=M[j][i], M[j][i]=M[i][j]. This is incorrect because M[i][j] is overwritten with garbage (t) before the original value is saved. B and D are eliminated.
steps.note: The first statement in a swap must always save the value to be overwritten.

steps.title: Distinguish A vs C: starting index of j
steps.formula_raw: Option A: j from 0; Option C: j from i
steps.apply: Option A (j starts from 0): Consider i=0, j=1 -> swap M[0][1] with M[1][0]. Later, i=1, j=0 -> swap M[1][0] with M[0][1] again, undoing the first swap. The final matrix would be unchanged, not transposed. Option C (j starts from i): For each i, j ranges from i to 3. This visits each pair (i,j) with j>=i exactly once. Off-diagonal elements (i!=j) are swapped exactly once, diagonal stays. Result is correct transpose.
steps.note: Option C also handles the diagonal (j=i) with no net effect since swapping M[i][i] with itself is a no-op.

solution.result: The correct replacement for X is option C: for(j = i; j < 4; ++j){ t = M[i][j]; M[i][j] = M[j][i]; M[j][i] = t; }

questions.formula_note: In-place matrix transpose: swap M[i][j] with M[j][i] for all i <= j to visit each off-diagonal pair exactly once and avoid double-swapping.
